summary: In his IPO letter to investors, Mark Zuckerberg outlines Facebook's foundational mission to make the world more open and connected. He details 'The Hacker Way,' a culture of continuous iteration, meritocracy, and speed, and explains his philosophy that Facebook makes money to build better services, not the other way around.

principles.statement: We make money to build better services, not the other way around.
principles.rationale: Building a strong economic engine is the best way to align people to solve important problems and attract great talent.
principles.source_quote: Simply put: we don’t build services to make money; we make money to build better services.

principles.statement: Focus on solving the most important problems to maximize impact.
principles.rationale: Most companies do this poorly and waste time; identifying the biggest problems ensures the greatest impact.
principles.source_quote: If we want to have the biggest impact, the best way to do this is to make sure we always focus on solving the most important problems.

principles.statement: A more open world is a better world.
principles.rationale: When people have more information, they can make better decisions and have a greater impact on society.
principles.source_quote: We believe that a more open world is a better world because people with more information can make better decisions and have a greater impact.

frameworks.name: The Hacker Way
frameworks.when_to_apply: When building products, managing teams, and scaling a company culture.
steps: Release small iterations quickly rather than trying to get everything right at once.
Prototype ideas immediately instead of debating them for days.
Foster an open meritocracy where the best idea and implementation win, regardless of hierarchy.
Continuously improve and iterate, believing that nothing is ever complete.
frameworks.source_quote: The Hacker Way is an approach to building that involves continuous improvement and iteration.

mental_models.name: Social by Design
mental_models.description: The idea that products built around people and their social connections are inherently more engaging and successful than traditional, isolated products.
mental_models.example: Disruptive new approaches in industries like games, music, and news that leverage the developer platform to integrate social features.

mental_models.name: Information Infrastructure as a Social Graph
mental_models.description: Viewing the ideal flow of global information as a bottom-up, peer-to-peer network rather than a monolithic, top-down structure.
mental_models.example: Giving people the power to share directly, which bypasses intermediaries and makes governments more responsive to citizens.

heuristics: The riskiest thing is to take no risks.

signature_quotes.text: Simply put: we don’t build services to make money; we make money to build better services.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the relationship between Facebook's social mission and its business model.

signature_quotes.text: The riskiest thing is to take no risks.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the core value of being bold.
Explaining the core value of moving fast to build and learn quickly.
Painted on Facebook's walls to remind employees to always keep shipping.
A hacker mantra emphasizing prototyping over debating.

signature_quotes.text: Facebook was not originally created to be a company. It was built to accomplish a social mission — to make the world more open and connected.
signature_quotes.context: The opening line of the IPO letter.

anti_patterns.description: Slowing down out of fear of making mistakes.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: You lose opportunities by moving too slowly; if you never break anything, you're probably not moving fast enough.
In a world that is changing so quickly, avoiding risk guarantees failure.

anti_patterns.description: Debating for days whether a new idea is possible.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It wastes time; it is much more effective to just prototype something and see what works.
